abstract: An abundance of evidence demonstrates that United Nations (UN) peacekeeping operations (PKOs) enhance the prospects for peace in conflict and post-conflict settings. Yet existing work also suggests that peacekeeping succeeds at some tasks while failing at others—often within the same mission. We argue that UN peacekeepers cannot eliminate all forms of violence because actions that reduce one type of violence create conditions that contribute to an increase in another type—a phenomenon we call the peacekeeping dilemma. Two countervailing mechanisms generate this dilemma: a deterrence mechanism, through which peacekeepers raise the costs of violence for non-state actors, and a collaboration mechanism, through which the UN's reliance on host-government consent facilitates government violence against civilians. We test this framework using a geographic regression discontinuity design around the border of Mali and Burkina Faso, where the UN mission MINUSMA operated from 2013 until its forced withdrawal in 2023. We find that UN peacekeeping reduces conflict between non-state rebel groups and communal violence between civilians, but not violence between governments and rebels. More troubling, we find evidence that UN peacekeeping increases government violence against civilians.

title: A systematic review of social science studies analyzing social media data, 2010-2024
authors: Kai-Cheng Yang, Pranav Goel, Meredith L. Pruden, Qunfang Wu, Colby Eagan, David Lazer, Deen Freelon
abstract: Social media platforms have long served as important data sources for social scientists. Policies around academic access to platform data, however, have seen substantial changes in recent years. These shifts have introduced profound uncertainty for scholarship relying on social media data. We evaluate the potential research impact of such changes by conducting a systematic analysis of scholarship published between 2010 and 2024 in 59 journals, representing five social science disciplines as well as leading interdisciplinary journals. Using a multi-method design that includes human-in-the-loop AI annotation, we find that 0.78% of papers in our corpus (3.15% when focusing exclusively on social science journals) use social media data for empirical research, with substantial variance across disciplines—ranging from less than 1% in Economics to approximately 13% in Communication. Twitter/X and Facebook are, respectively, the first and second most popular data sources across all disciplines, throughout the 15 years of our study. Further, the percentage of papers using social media data has dramatically increased over this period, tripling its share of social science publications, although this share has plateaued and even declined in the last few years. This recent decline has been driven by Twitter/X and Facebook in particular, and suggests that the empirical study of social media may be on the cusp of significant changes. These changes might affect the study of real-time events and phenomena with widespread sociopolitical ramifications, such as the COVID-19 pandemic and misleading information surrounding the 2016 U.S. presidential election, which we observe to be some of the major topics studied by social scientists using social media data.

title: The empirically inscrutable climate-economy relationship
abstract: Empirical models of the macro-level climate-economy relationship project climate change impacts ranging from trivial to catastrophic. This wide range suggests identification challenges. Here, we show that such models indeed face identification challenges that are axiomatic and irreducible. The climate-economy relationship has qualitatively important variation across space and time, due to complex regional heterogeneity and affluence-driven adaptation, among other factors. Empirical models must assume away some of this variation to preserve degrees of freedom. Influential observations caused by economic disasters and miracles, along with low signal-to-noise ratios, create additional challenges. We illustrate these challenges theoretically and then empirically by replicating and sensitivity testing prominent climate-econometric studies. The estimation challenges create economically meaningful sensitivities and fragilities. We recommend a cautious and uncertainty-emphasizing approach to applying climate econometrics to public and private decision-making. Importantly, however, our analysis does not dismiss the existence of a negative carbon externality nor economically beneficial efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

title: Childrearing Ideals, Autonomy, and Care in Comparative Perspective
abstract: Across societies, adults vary markedly in the qualities they believe children should learn at home. This paper examines how autonomy‑oriented versus relational childrearing ideals relate to beliefs about individual responsibility, family care obligations, and subjective well‑being. Using World Values Survey data from over 100 countries, I construct an Autonomy–Care Gap (ACG) capturing the relative emphasis on achievement‑oriented versus relational child qualities. Decomposing the ACG into within‑ and between‑country components reveals distinct patterns. Within countries, shifts toward greater autonomy orientation are associated with stronger endorsement of individual responsibility, weaker family care norms, and slightly lower life satisfaction. Across countries, however, these associations vary with institutional context: in settings with stronger support for government responsibility, autonomy‑oriented childrearing does not correspond to more individualistic beliefs. A cohort exposure design finds no evidence that exposure to autonomy‑oriented childrearing climates during childhood durably shapes adult attitudes. Instead, responsibility beliefs are more strongly associated with contemporaneous cultural environments. The findings suggest that autonomy can be embedded in divergent institutional arrangements and that beliefs about responsibility remain responsive to social and institutional contexts over the life course.

title: The Status Foundations of Conspiracy Beliefs
abstract: Prior survey research has mostly centered on the psychological dispositions and political leanings associated with conspiracy beliefs rather than underlying and potentially consequential status dynamics. Drawing on prior scholarship and recent national survey data, I analyze the social patterning of conspiracy beliefs and their variations by several status attributes. Notably, and rather than the typical assumption that such beliefs are mostly held by those of lower education, my findings point clearly to a bimodal (U-shaped) distribution by socioeconomic status. Specifically, and unique to my results, there exists a cluster of graduate-degree-holding white men who display a penchant for conspiracy beliefs. Further analyses highlight important variation between specific beliefs, with distinctly taboo beliefs being exceptionally popular among those in this highly educated group—a pattern corroborated with additional data sources. I conclude by discussing potential mechanisms and avenues that future sociological work on conspiracy beliefs might consider.

title: The Political Landscape of AI-assisted Elite Fact-Checking on Social Media
abstract: We investigate the emerging practice of AI-assisted on-platform fact-checking, where ordinary social media users employ large language model (LLM) agents to publicly scrutinize elite claims in real time. Focusing on the platform X, we explore how users leverage the Grok LLM to evaluate posts made by American elites. By analyzing an original dataset of N=1,346,716 posts by 1,749 elites between 2025-03-07 and 2025-10-06 using text-as-data methods, crowdsourced annotations, and fixed effect linear models, we find that the content of the post and the perceived ideological orientation of the elite posting it are primary predictors of what gets scrutinized. Specifically, posts that concern a political topic are more likely to be fact-checked. At the account level, elites perceived to be more political and more right-leaning are more likely to be fact-checked. However, the ideological asymmetry is largely driven by a few elites with disproportionate influence in the platform. Our findings help characterize user-initiated, real-time fact-checking as a novel technological affordance that fundamentally alters the institution of fact-checking. We show that it manifests as a non-neutral process that is shaped by partisan perceptions and has implications for digital accountability and epistemic authority on online platforms.

title: An investigation of the determinants of survey enjoyment in an online probability panel
authors: Katya Kostadintcheva, Patrick Sturgis, Jouni Kuha
abstract: We assess how key features of the survey experience and characteristics of respondents are related to survey enjoyment, with a particular focus on questionnaire topic and the direct and moderating effects of respondent personality. Beyond the intrinsic value of promoting surveys that are more enjoyable for the public to participate in, understanding what shapes whether and how respondents enjoy completing surveys matters instrumentally for minimising nonresponse and maximising data quality. To address these questions, we model respondent self-reports of survey enjoyment over repeated waves of an online probability panel (OPP). Our findings show that the topics included in the questionnaire are a key determinant, with the probability of enjoyment varying substantially across topic areas. Enjoyment of the survey is also influenced by respondent personality, survey mode, and how interested respondents are in these topics ex ante.

title: Dimensions of lexical mastery and their relationships with listening and reading among beginner-to-low-intermediate learners of French
abstract: The extent to which lexical mastery consists of interrelated types of lexical knowledge has been widely discussed. Yet, few studies have explored whether the lexical dimensions of knowledge and processing are relatively independent or their contribution to listening and reading, particularly among beginner-to-low-intermediate learners of languages other than English. The current study examines the extent to which lexical knowledge and processing are (a) relatively independent and (b) predict listening and reading. 218 beginner-to-low intermediate adolescent learners of French completed a battery of tests, yielding measures of high-frequency lexical knowledge (meaning recognition, form recognition, and form recall), lexical processing (speed, stability, and automaticity of word recognition), and listening and reading proficiency. Results suggested that lexical knowledge and processing were relatively independent at this stage of proficiency. Lexical knowledge (but not processing) was found to strongly predict listening and reading. These findings underscore the importance of high-frequency vocabulary at these early stages.

title: Asymmetric Assimilation or Mutual Adaptation? Intermarriage and Ethnic Disparities in Women’s Full-Time Homemaking in China, 1982–2015
abstract: Despite progress toward gender equality in China, recent decades have witnessed a resurgence of patriarchal values, reflected in a growing number of women leaving the labor force to become full-time homemakers. Using census data from 1982 to 2015, this study examines ethnic variations in this trend, with a particular focus on interethnic marriages. Results reveal an overall increase in homemaking that varies across ethnic groups: women from Manchu, Mongolian, and Muslim groups (Hui, Kazakh, Uyghur) are more likely to be full-time homemakers than Han majority women, whereas Korean, Tibetan, and Southern minority women exhibit lower probabilities. Analysis of intermarriage reveals asymmetric assimilation: Han women’s homemaking rates remain relatively stable regardless of spousal ethnicity, while minority women’s rates converge toward Han norms. Among groups whose traditional norms favor homemaking (e.g., Manchu, Mongolian, Muslim), higher education and greater prefecture-level Han presence amplify the declines in homemaking associated with intermarriage. Among groups with historically higher labor force participation (e.g., Korean, Tibetan), higher education mitigates increases in homemaking linked to intermarriage, while Han concentration exerts minimal impact. These findings suggest that asymmetric assimilation arises from a complex interplay of adaptation and resistance to Han cultural norms, offering insights into the social processes shaping gender and ethnic stratification in contemporary China.

title: Towards a Post-Social Media Studies
abstract: For two decades, "social media" has been the master lens through which scholars have understood digital communication—an era defined by user-generated content, networked publics, and participatory culture. That era is drawing to a close. This paper argues that three interrelated dynamics are dissolving the social media paradigm: an algorithmic shift from social-graph-based to interest-based recommendation, which is remaking the active "user" into a passive "viewer"; the generative AI revolution, which is replacing user-generated content with synthetic media and decoupling platforms from any dependence on human participation; and an exodus from public platforms toward private, closed spaces. Together, these dynamics are giving rise to three distinct post-social formations: algorithmically governed broadcasting platforms, semi-private spheres and micro-communities, and AI-mediated communication as a new media form in its own right. Understanding this transformation demands a fundamental reorientation of the field—new conceptual tools that move beyond networked publics and participatory culture, new methods suited to synthetic and ephemeral media environments, and renewed attention to the political stakes of communication systems that no longer require human participation.

title: Wealth as Private Insurance. Unequal Protection against the Scarring Effects of Unemployment in the U.S. and Germany
authors: Martin Ehlert, Fabian T. Pfeffer, Samuel Jalalian
abstract: Job loss has detrimental and long-lasting effects on workers, including lower wages after reemployment. The size of these wage scars, however, depends on the safety nets available during unemployment. Whereas prior research has focused on public safety nets, particularly unemployment insurance, we extend this perspective by considering the private safety net provided by household wealth. Because access to wealth is unequally distributed, private insurance may reinforce the stratifying effects of unemployment on subsequent wage inequality. Comparing the United States and Germany allows us to assess how private wealth interacts with public insurance in buffering the effects of job loss. Drawing on two high-quality panel surveys (PSID and SOEP) and difference-in-differences estimators we show that in the United States, wage scars are larger among workers with little wealth than among workers with greater wealth, producing pronounced inequality in post-unemployment outcomes. We find no comparable pattern in Germany, suggesting that wealth becomes an important safety net primarily where public insurance is weak. Additional analyses show that even in the United States, household wealth protects only the wealthiest households and only during shorter unemployment spells. The effect of wealth on wage scars appears to operate mainly through differences in job-search strategies.

title: Spirit-Master Beliefs and Cooperation Among the Buryat of Mongolia
authors: Daniel Major-Smith, Benjamin Grant Purzycki, Byambabaatar Ichinkhorloo
abstract: Previous cross-cultural research suggests that beliefs in punitive and omniscient deities may promote cooperation among distant co-religionists. However, much of this work is limited by: i) predominantly focusing on “moralistic” religious traditions (e.g., Christianity, Hinduism), rather the beliefs towards less moralistic “local” deities; and ii) not taking a formal causal inference approach, leaving potential causal interpretations unclear. We aimed to overcome these limitations here among the Buryat of eastern Mongolia. Our focal estimand was the joint causal effect of punishment and omniscience beliefs regarding local deities (spirit-masters) on cooperation towards distant co-religionists of the same local religious tradition. Cooperation was measured using two experimental games, the Random Allocation and Dictator Games, with distant co-religionists the target recipients. Overall, results were consistent with there being no relationship between spirit-master beliefs and cooperation towards distant co-religionists. Assuming a causal interpretation, these results suggest that – in this Inner Asian population – local punishment and omniscience beliefs regarding spirit-masters do not promote cooperation with distant co-religionists. However, as cooperation was overwhelmingly fair, there may have been insufficient variation in behaviour to detect meaningful differences by religious beliefs (or anything else).

title: The multidimensional influence of health on fertility outcomes for men: evidence from Swedish military conscription data
abstract: We examine how health in early adulthood predicts men’s later-life fertility using Swedish population registers linked to military conscription examinations. We study men born 1951–1979, observed for fertility through 2024, and exploit objective health measured at ages 18–20 across twelve indicators: muscular strength (handgrip, arm, leg, combined), cardiovascular health (resting heart rate, blood pressure, ECG pathology, maximal working capacity on cycle ergometer), and blood/urine biomarkers (hematocrit, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, U-glucose, U-albumin). Outcomes are completed parity, childlessness, ever-marrying, and childlessness among the ever-married. We estimate linear models for all men and sibling fixed-effects models that compare full brothers. Men in the lowest strength and fitness deciles have 0.25–0.45 fewer children and a 10–20 percentage-point higher probability of childlessness than those in the highest deciles. High resting heart rate shows similar but slightly smaller gradients. Blood pressure categories and ECG abnormalities exhibit modest associations. Biomarkers indicative of metabolic and renal stress also have modest associations with fertility. Findings are consistent with multiple potential explanatory mechanisms and indicate that early-adult health is an important explanation for which men have children. We find particularly low fertility among those with the worst health, and that a substantial share of the association is driven by childlessness.

title: Pricing the Future: China’s Ambitions for Commodity Derivatives Markets
abstract: China’s efforts to develop commodity futures markets and enhance its influence over commodity pricing are best understood as part of a broader strategy to reduce financial vulnerabilities and increase strategic autonomy within a hierarchical global financial system. Commodity pricing power – defined as the capacity to shape the infrastructures, regulation, participation and currency denomination of derivative markets through which prices are formed – has become an increasingly important component of geopolitical competition. For China, this is not about economic efficiency, but control over the terms under which strategically important commodities are valued and traded.

title: Attitudes and barriers to open science practices: a mixed-methods analysis at a criminological research institute
authors: Asier Moneva, Wim Bernasco, Steve van de Weijer, Beate Volker, Amy Nivette
abstract: The open science movement strives to improve the transparency, accessibility, rigor, and reproducibility of scientific research. Arguing that open science increases the impact of research, the Dutch Research Council (NWO) has been promoting an open science program in the Netherlands since 2009, aiming to make all funded publications open access and research data open and FAIR, i.e. findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable. Our research institute, the Netherlands Institute for the Study of Crime and Law Enforcement (NSCR), is one of the ten national research institutes that receive long-term structural funding from the NWO. It has therefore also taken up the challenge of moving from a ‘closed’ to an ‘open’ model of science. If implemented in full, this cultural shift would require NSCR staff to relate to open science values in a nationwide system where recognizing and rewarding open science practices remains a challenge. In this paper, we use data from an online questionnaire among our colleagues to (1) describe NSCR scientific staff attitudes towards nine open science practices—publishing open access, publishing preprints, sharing open code, sharing open data, sharing open materials, conducting open peer review, using open source software, pre-registering research, and disclosing contribution roles—and (2) identify barriers to adopting these practices. We used a mixed methods approach combining descriptive quantitative analysis of 5-point Likert scale items with qualitative inductive thematic analysis of open-ended questions. This approach reveals a nuanced perspective on open science practices, overcoming the false dichotomy of all for or all against. Although attitudes towards the open science practices are generally positive, the thematic analysis also identifies 36 barriers that should be addressed to support their further adoption.

title: Young people's views on well-being and schooling in the post-pandemic era
authors: INVEST Flagship, Venla Jusslin, Mia Hakovirta, Eija Lindroos, Anu Lyytinen, Mikko Niemelä, Juuso Repo
abstract: This report examines the experiences of Finnish young people about the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic for well-being, schooling and future faith in the post-pandemic era. The data was collected as group interviews in the spring of 2025 (N = 29), in which young people over the age of 15 were asked to reflect on their own experiences in relation to the results of two studies (Niemelä & Laaninen 2024; Repo 2024) on the well-being of young people several years after the acute phase of the pandemic. Based on the results, the pandemic does not appear to be an unambiguous turning point in the well-being of young people. Instead, their experiences highlight individual life situations as well as post-pandemic social changes, such as economic uncertainty, cuts in social security, the consequences of the war in Ukraine, as well as general unpredictability. Young people's faith in the future and trust in society appear to have weakened in many ways in the post-pandemic era. Experiences in schooling were twofold: some young people benefited from the flexibility of distance education, while others felt that learning and support became more difficult, especially in the context of educational transitions. Distance education and exceptional arrangements were felt to have treated young people unequally. The results highlight the need to look at the well-being of young people in time, differentiating between the situation during and after a pandemic, and as part of the polycrisis of the early 2020s, where the challenges of well-being of young people are intertwined with the transitions of educational paths and wider societal uncertainty. The report is part of the Children First: Nordic policies and children´s wellbeing research project, which produces new scientific knowledge on the COVID-19 pandemic’s impact on children´s wellbeing and identifies policy options to enhance children´s wellbeing and resilience in Nordic countries. This three-year project (2023–2026) is carried out by a consortium of five universities: University of Turku (coordinator), University Iceland, Stockholm University, Uppsala University and Åbo Akademi University. The project is funded by NordForsk.

title: Child home care allowance take-up and subsequent childbearing
authors: INVEST Flagship, Anni Erlandsson, Tapio Räsänen
abstract: The child home care allowance, also referred as cash-for-care, is intended to give parents the option to extend their parental leave, stay home, and delay the placement of a small child in day care. Yet, the allowance is primarily used by mothers. In this study, we fill a research gap by examining how the duration of child home care allowance use is associated with birth spacing and the transition to higher parities – namely, second and third births in Finland. These outcomes are investigated for different groups based on, e.g., education level, earnings, and labour market status. Relying on full-population Finnish register data and survival analysis, we examine women who had their first child between 2000 and 2014. Our results show that home care allowance take-up was associated with a faster transition to a subsequent childbirth, but the association was positive only for mothers using the allowance for a short or a moderate period (1-18 months). Also, mothers using the home care allowance for a longer period (more than 18 months) were least likely to have a second or a third child compared to other mothers. Socioeconomic status, education, and labour market status before childbirth were linked to the length of the home care allowance period and parity progression. We show evidence that mothers from disadvantaged backgrounds, such as mothers with only primary education, low income or not in employment before childbirth were the most likely to take up the home care allowance – and stay home – for a prolonged period.

title: Missing Women on Indian Streets
abstract: How absent are women from city streets in the developing world? We answer this question using GPS-linked wearable cameras and randomized street audits across \textasciitilde 900 kilometers of roads in greater Mumbai. Across 4000+ street images containing 23,000+ visible person observations, women account for 16.4\% of visible people in Mumbai and 14.7\% in Navi Mumbai, far below their population shares. We estimate pedestrian sex ratios of 239 and 223 women per 1,000 men, implying 71\% and 76\% of women expected based on residential ratios are \textit{missing} from the streets. This pattern holds across road types, and private mobility does not explain the gap; women's share on two-wheelers is lower still (8.4\% and 5.7\%). These results provide the first large-scale measurement of gender disparities in urban public life that self-reported data cannot capture.

title: Depression by Default: A Hypothesis Paper Proposing a Depression-Oriented Explanatory Model for Observed Failures in Chronic Pain Assessment
abstract: Chronic pain affects approximately one in five adults globally and represents the leading cause of years lived with disability worldwide. Despite the IASP's three-category mechanistic taxonomy — nociceptive, neuropathic, and nociplastic — the clinical infrastructure of chronic pain management has developed in ways that position depression-oriented instruments, treatment algorithms, and clinical vocabularies as primary rather than conditional. Hypothesis: This paper proposes an explanatory model — the depression-default orientation — holding that an overgeneralization of the affective overlap between pain and depression into etiological and treatment equivalence has produced a convergent pattern of assessment failures across instruments, first-line treatment sequencing, and clinical training. The model predicts specific, testable mismatches between pain mechanism type and clinical response that are consistent with existing evidence and addressable by prospective research. Existing Evidence: The Cochrane Network Meta-Analysis by Birkinshaw et al. (2023), examining 176 RCTs across 28,664 participants, found antidepressant efficacy in only 11 of 42 chronic pain comparisons — a pattern consistent with a deployment mismatch rather than evidence of general antidepressant utility across pain populations. Neuroimaging and systems neuroscience literature is consistent with a distinction between pain's primary pathways and depression's primary pathways that the depression-default orientation does not adequately reflect. Testable Predictions: The model generates falsifiable predictions including elevated false-positive depression rates in pain-origin populations using somatic-inclusive screening tools, differential treatment outcomes by pain mechanism type under depression-first vs. mechanistic-first intake protocols, and measurable depression-framework bias in pain management training curricula and clinical guidelines. Proposed Framework: A four-step mechanistic intake model is proposed as a testable corrective, sequencing pain origin history and provisional IASP classification before instrument deployment. Conclusion: The depression-default orientation, if confirmed as a systematic pattern by prospective research, would represent a correctable source of assessment failure affecting a substantial proportion of the chronic pain population. The hypothesis is offered as a framework for that investigation.

title: Dignity at the Door: Structural Failures in Chronic Pain Intake and a Framework for Agency-Centered Care
abstract: The chronic pain intake encounter is conventionally framed as a triage event — an administrative threshold between referral and treatment. This commentary argues it is something else: a neurologically and psychologically formative moment occurring in a population whose accumulated clinical history constitutes heightened receptivity to whatever framework the clinical system delivers. Drawing on the nocebo literature, schema formation research, and the psychophysiology of high-activation states, this paper proposes that the framing, instruments, and default interventions introduced at intake do not merely describe the patient's situation — they begin to constitute it. Three structural features of current intake practice are examined. First, the Pain Catastrophizing Scale, whose construct validity at the item level is examined in companion work, may not reliably distinguish accurate functional reporting from maladaptive cognitive distortion in patients whose functional losses reflect verifiable physical reality. Second, the durability evidence for cognitive behavioral therapy — the field's predominant psychological intervention — shows substantially more variable long-term effects than end-of-treatment profiles suggest, with the weakest performance in the populations most frequently referred. Third, the antidepressant default in chronic pain management substantially exceeds its highest-quality evidence base: the 2023 Cochrane network meta-analysis found no high-certainty evidence for any antidepressant in any chronic pain condition, yet prescribing patterns remain broad. The communicative consequences of these structural defaults — in a population characterized by heightened neurological suggestibility — are argued to produce durable schemas of helplessness and external dependency that compound rather than address treatment-refractory presentations. A four-principle framework for agency-centered intake is proposed: truth in measurement, genuine hope grounded in mechanism rather than reassurance, patient visibility as primary clinical data, and agency-framing from the first encounter. The framework requires no new funding, no new billing codes, and no dismantling of existing care structures. It requires sequencing interventions differently, measuring more completely, and communicating with awareness that the intake window carries disproportionate formative weight. Outcomes research comparing standard intake to framework-guided intake is identified as the next empirical requirement. Keywords: chronic pain intake; agency-centered care; nocebo effect; Pain Catastrophizing Scale; cognitive behavioral therapy durability; antidepressant evidence; patient dignity; central sensitization; locus of control; schema formation

title: The Wrong Framework: How Opioid Restriction Policy Fails the Patients It Was Never Designed to Protect
abstract: The opioid crisis produced a sweeping policy response that reduced prescription opioid manufacturing by more than 68% for oxycodone and 73% for hydrocodone since 2015. This response was built around an assumed patient profile — one driven by addiction, reward-seeking, and psychological vulnerability. The evidence suggests that this profile does not accurately describe the majority of patients for whom opioids are legitimately prescribed in chronic pain populations. Objective: To evaluate whether current opioid restriction frameworks appropriately align with the clinical and epidemiological characteristics of the populations they most directly affect. Core Argument: The opioid restriction apparatus treats two clinically distinct pathways as a single population: patients who arrive at opioid use through depression and psychological suffering (depression-first) and patients who arrive through documented nociceptive pathology (pain-first). These pathways are not categorical; they overlap in real clinical populations and co-occur frequently. However, their dominant relationships to opioid exposure, addiction risk, and policy consequences differ in ways the current framework does not distinguish. Evidence: Chronic pain patients represent fewer than 2% of all suicides by opioid overdose. They are, however, at substantially elevated risk of suicide relative to the general population — with meta-analyses reporting doubled-to-tripled rates and specific subpopulations reporting higher — and they die predominantly by firearm. More than two-thirds of chronic pain suicide decedents identified their pain as a direct contributor to their crisis. Illicit fentanyl now accounts for approximately 90% of current opioid overdose deaths; prescription opioid deaths — the original target of restriction — totaled 13,026 in 2023. Much of the evidence in this area is observational; causal interpretation is limited, though consistency across multiple cohorts suggests clinically meaningful associations. Conclusion: The opioid restriction framework is producing measurable harm in populations it was not calibrated to address, while the populations it was designed to address are dying of illicit drugs the restriction does not reach. This paper proposes differentiated intake screening to distinguish pain-first from depression-first patients, enabling clinically appropriate prescribing decisions and accurate crisis measurement. Perspective This article argues that the opioid restriction apparatus conflates two clinically distinct chronic pain populations with different relationships to opioid exposure and overdose risk. It proposes differentiated intake screening as the corrective first step, enabling policy to reach the patients it was built for while no longer harming those it was not. Keywords: chronic pain; opioid restriction; addiction risk; screening framework; opioid use disorder

title: Local community opposition to data centers is clear and growing
authors: Mason Reece, Adam Rauh, Tyler Simko
abstract: Rapid growth in computing and generative artificial intelligence is creating unprecedented demand for data center construction. Anecdotal media reports suggest residents often resist local governments approving data centers in their communities, citing concerns over issues like water consumption and expected increases in utility costs. However, studying the political reactions of communities across the United States to data centers remains difficult due to a lack of centralized data on local policymaking. We offer the first large-scale evidence of how U.S. communities discuss and respond to data center expansion by collecting and transcribing videos of local government meetings between 2007 and 2026. We show that the primary tension in local policymaking is between the economic development potential of data centers and expected local costs, such as noise, utility rate increases, and environmental impacts. We also find that residents are systematically less supportive of data centers than officials when discussing the same proposals.

title: Wasta as Informal Governance: A Componential and Institutional Theory for Emerging Market Economies
abstract: Wasta is not simply a cultural practice but an ingrained cognitive model that shapes how people evaluate justice, responsibility, & moral action. Wasta illustrates a cultural mode of mental reasoning in honour-based, relational, interdependent societies where cognition is holistic, and decision-making prioritises network-based trust, obligation, & loyalty over impartial rules. Wasta in the MENA region and other collectivist societies—in general understood by scholars as the use of personal relationships to secure advantages—remains insufficiently researched, despite its pervasive influence across formal organisational systems and governance in emerging markets. Existing research often treats the Wasta phenomenon as a fragmented concept, often combined with nepotism or favouritism, thereby limiting Wasta measurement and analytical clarity. This research study analysis develops the Componential and Institutional Wasta Theory (CIWT), a multidimensional framework that conceptualises Wasta as an informal governance mechanism ingrained in institutional and socio-cultural dynamics. Drawing on qualitative evidence from observations, focus groups, interviews, and prior literature, the examination identifies five critical core elements (i.e., factors or roots) that structure Wasta practices and illustrates their differential influence on organisational performance and outcomes, including creativity, innovation, corruption, institutional trust, and employee well-being. The proposed Wasta conceptual framework extends prior studies and advances the literature in three ways: (1) the conceptual framework provides researchers with a unified and agreed-on theoretical definition of Wasta, (2) it presents a measurable componential structure promoting future empirical analysis, and (3) it links informal Wasta practices to formal organisational or institutional performance. By explaining, describing, and clarifying how Wasta functions across formal and informal business and social contexts, the newly developed theory and its conceptual framework offer actionable insights for policymakers, business leaders, and other NGO organisations striving to mitigate bribery, inequality, and corruption and reinforce governance in emerging market economies.

title: After Labor and Capital The Political Economy of the Transitional Period
abstract: This manuscript argues that information should no longer be treated merely as one factor among others, but as a metafactor that increasingly reorganizes labor, capital, institutions, and growth. The central claim is that contemporary economies are entering a transition period in which the cost of generating signals, models, texts, and decisions falls much faster than the cost of verifying them. As a result, the decisive constraint gradually shifts from production to selection, validation, and institutional recognition. To formalize this shift, the book introduces the concept of the verification bottleneck, κ, understood not as a sector-specific obstacle but as a general structural limit on the conversion of available information into economically effective knowledge, I∗. On this basis, the manuscript reinterprets major traditions of economic thought as partial descriptions of one broader informational process and develops implications for labor displacement, the changing nature of capital, platform power, growth regimes, and the political economy of verification. The argument is supported by a formal appendix and an empirical appendix combining cross-country evidence, platform-era labor-share dynamics, and new indicators related to verification capacity. The broader conclusion is that the main conflict of the transition period no longer concerns only ownership of capital or control of labor, but increasingly the control of infrastructures and procedures through which signals acquire the status of legitimate knowledge.

title: The Underdevelopment Curve: Structural Framework for Economies Stuck on Underdevelopment
abstract: The process of Underdevelopment is naturally in many cases causal, many Countries on developing world in fact they comproved this evidence, when underdevelopment are characterized as causal evidence, normally influence strongly the trajectory of the Country. On this approach, in particular, analyse for example some important factors that contributed, first to identified the stagnation on the Country and on other hand to analyse for example the main reason that keep the main Countries trapped on underdevelopment world. This approach in fact analyse the reasons that influence developing Countries to not get prosperity, the study for example, comproved that the Underdevelopment in many cases are causal, from some Inefficiencies that are for example prompted from inexistense of alignment of basics conditions and transformation levels that are normally needed. The study show for example, that natural resources not are effectively enough to introduce prosperity and not are enough to change the destiny of the Country, many on large majority Countries with high economic potencial quantified from natural resources, not drive respective economies to prosperity. On other hand, on this approach, the study provided the some important reasons that normally not are enough to contributed to guarantee high growth, in fact, exist many divergencies between countries that have many Basics Conditions and with Countries that not have, example between Japan and Venezuela and Democratic Republic of Congo, reinforce this relationship. In particular, the underdevelopment curve, show the mechanisms that are normally used from developing Countries, for example, exist some Countries with high levels of basic conditions including natural resources, on other hand these Countries are continuously trapped on underdevelopment. Some Countries such as China and India, follow for example the trajectory that was followed by Japan. From Latin America, Africa, Southeast Asia, countries continuously trapped in underdevelopment.

title: TWO-LEVEL OPEN-SOURCE ARCHITECTURE FOR INDEPENDENT AI RANKING OF SCIENTIFIC JOURNALS
abstract: Introduction. Existing journal ranking systems measure citations but not the actual content of articles. A high impact factor may be driven by names of influential authors, intra-journal self-citation, or trendy topics lacking genuine novelty, while a young, high-quality journal remains outside official rankings. Objective. To develop and test an open-source two-level architecture for independent journal ranking based on an author's calibrated AI reviewer. Methodology. At the first level, five random articles from an issue of each journal are assessed against five criteria (novelty, methodology, practical value, visualization, ethics) to identify the strongest, median, and weakest articles. At the second level, peer articles from all journals are compared within a single context. The experiment was conducted on five Russian journals from a fixed subject area. All materials are anonymized; the prompt, data, and code are open for replication. Results. The journals are clearly differentiated by quality: two journals (J4, J3) demonstrate a comparable level (mean scores 68.9–75.6), one occupies a middle position (62.3 points), and two journals (J5 and J2) consistently hold outsider positions (51.15–51.2). Internal heterogeneity of journals and diagnostic discrepancies with traditional scientometric indicators were revealed. Conclusion. The proposed architecture provides independent, reproducible journal evaluation without subscription to commercial databases. Limitations include the disciplinary specificity of the prompt and the need for open access to articles.

title: The Value of Hydropower as a Grid-Scale Storage Resource: A Commodity Market Approach
authors: Brittany Tarufelli, James Gibson, Sarah Barrows, Abhishek Somani
abstract: Energy storage plays a crucial role in modern power systems, providing services such as grid stability, frequency regulation, and load balancing. However, as storage deployment expands, arbitrage profitability declines due to market saturation, pointing to the need for new business models that more fully compensate energy storage. This paper presents empirical research on the value of energy storage using a commodity-based framework. We examine hydropower as a proxy for grid-scale storage, using exogenous variation in reservoir storage volume to estimate causal effects of storage on (1) real-time electricity prices and (2) risk premiums, measured by the day-ahead to real-time price spread, in the Northwestern United States between May 2022 and November 2024. Employing fixed-effects and lagged dependent variable models, we find that a 10% increase in reservoir storage volume reduces real-time prices by approximately 6% and lowers risk premiums by about 5%.

title: Psychosocial predictors and justification patterns of traditional and alternative medicine in Peru
authors: Angelo Fasce, José Eduardo Rosales Trabuco, Javier Rodríguez-Ferreiro, Itxaso Barberia, Elvis Pinedo-Yzaguirre, Juan Manuel Espinoza Nuñez, Wilson Marcos Ortiz-Treviños, Christian David Pizarro Moncada, Mario Reyes-Bossio, Carlos Carbajal-León
abstract: This mixed-methods study investigates the prevalence, attitudes, psychological predictors, and usage reasons of traditional and alternative medicine in Peru. A representative national sample (N = 805) reported their experiences with 16 unconventional medical techniques, alongside psychometric measures of pseudoscientific beliefs, ideological traditionalism, and trust in the medical profession. Traditional medicine was more widely used, while alternative medicine elicited more favorable attitudinal responses. Pseudoscientific beliefs predicted greater endorsement of both modalities, with traditionalism specifically linked to traditional medicine. To enrich the psychometric findings, we applied natural language processing techniques to 2716 usage reasons extracted from spontaneous open-ended responses, revealing distinct rhetorical patterns: alternative medicine was more often justified through pseudoscientific and biomedical-sounding arguments, whereas traditional medicine drew on cultural, spiritual, and experiential narratives. Network and co-occurrence analyses confirmed that both modalities follow distinctive general combinatory logics of justification. However, subgroups mixing traditional and alternative medicine also emerged, such as spiritually oriented practices and techniques pivoting traditionalist conceptions. These findings reflect the interplay between global health discourses and local belief systems. Overall, this study highlights the value of integrating cultural and linguistic analyses to understand public engagement with unconventional health practices.

title: Customizable Bayesian Adaptive Testing with Python – The adaptivetesting Package
authors: Jonas Engicht, R. Maximilian Bee, Tobias Koch
abstract: This paper introduces an open-source Python package for simplified, customizable Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT) using Bayesian methods. It addresses the lack of sophisticated packages for CAT in the Python programming language. Moreover, it bridges the gap between the construction and simulation of adaptive tests and their practical application by providing a dedicated API for integration with experiment software. Thereby, it eliminates the need of major code rewrites when transitioning from simulated to real-world adaptive testing. By leveraging Python’s object-oriented programming approach, such as abstract classes, protocols, and inheritance, the package allows for easy extension and customization of its functionality. For example, Bayesian estimators can be modified to incorporate custom priors. This paper outlines the relevance and practical use of the adaptivetesting package through a walkthrough example. The package is fully documented, and its source code is published on GitHub. It is also available on the Python Package Index (PyPi) thus it can easily be installed using Python’s package manager pip. Leveraging R’s reticulate package, adaptivetesting can also be accessed from within RStudio.

title: Intra-Individual Variability in Children's Self-Regulation in Kindergarten Classrooms: An Observational Study
abstract: Children’s ability to self-regulate varies across days and contextual demands. However, the predominant approach in developmental science is to assess self-regulation at a single timepoint in one context, limiting our understanding of how and why children’s self-regulation skills vary intra- and inter-individually. The present study addresses this gap by repeatedly observing children’s self-regulation in the classroom 10 times during two different classroom activities. The sample consisted of N = 201 Swiss kindergarteners (47.8% female, M = 5.4y., SD = 0.6). We assessed children’s self-regulation using an observational measure that captures two self-regulation dimensions: attention control and inhibitory control. To analyze self-regulation variability, we used multilevel location-scale models in a dynamic structural equation modeling framework. The findings indicated substantial intra-individual variability in self-regulation across the 10 classroom observations. Moreover, children differed significantly in the degree of intra-individual variability. When controlling for class membership, differences in attention control variability were related to the teacher-student relationship closeness. Differences in inhibitory control variability were associated with children’s gender. Overall, the present findings indicate that young children’s self-regulation skills can vary substantially across days and classroom activities. These findings emphasize the importance of considering the role of momentary regulatory demands in children’s self-regulation capacities.

title: The Signal-To-Noise Ratio Hypothesis of Intelligence
authors: Klaus Oberauer, Anna-Lena Schubert, Gidon T. Frischkorn, Michael D. Nunez, Christian Fiebach
abstract: If there is a common cause explaining the positive manifold underlying general intelligence, it is likely to be a feature of the cognitive system that affects its performance globally, irrespective of what type of information the system processes and for what purpose. We propose that the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of cognitive processing is such a global determinant of individual differences. The SNR affects the fidelity of every information-processing system. Therefore, if there were a stable and general SNR of the cognitive system, it would affect performance in virtually all cognitive tasks, rendering it a good candidate for explaining the general factor of intelligence. In computational models of cognition, individual differences in performance are driven by differences in model parameters. A general characteristic of the cognitive system that affects performance broadly should be reflected as a parameter that is shared by models of the cognitive activities that contribute to the positive manifold. Models of simple decisions, sensory discrimination, categorization, decision making, learning, and working memory have little in common, but they all share one thing: They have a parameter that reflects the SNR. For at least two classes of models – evidence accumulation models of simple decisions, and models of working memory – the SNR parameter is substantially correlated with fluid intelligence, providing initial evidence for this novel SNR hypothesis of intelligence.

title: A PYTHON FRAMEWORK FOR ANALYZING DEPENDENT-VARIABLE LAWS OF LARGE NUMBERS CONVERGENCE: TESTING SECTORAL SURPLUS VALUE RATE UNIFORMITY
abstract: This paper develops a comprehensive Python framework for analyzing the convergence of sectoral surplus value rates through multiple approaches to the Law of Large Numbers (LLN), specifically addressing dependent-variable scenarios. We implement and compare three distinct methodologies: triangular arrays, weighted sums with dependent variables, and mixingale processes, each offering unique insights into different aspects of convergence behavior. Our framework incorporates flexible convergence thresholds, detailed difference analysis, and sophisticated Excel reporting capabilities. The results reveal a complex pattern of sectoral convergence. While traditional approaches (triangular arrays and mixingale processes) indicate persistent sectoral differences, the weighted sums method, which explicitly accounts for inter-sectoral correlations, shows evidence of convergence at certain thresholds. This divergence in results suggests the existence of what we term “network uniformity” - a phenomenon where sectors, while maintaining individual characteristics, exhibit systematic convergence patterns when their interconnections are properly weighted. Our findings challenge conventional interpretations of sectoral rate uniformity, suggesting that modern economies might exhibit more sophisticated forms of convergence than traditionally theorized. The framework demonstrates that understanding sectoral relationships, rather than individual sector behaviors, is crucial for accurate economic analysis. These results have significant implications for economic policy and forecasting, particularly in highly interconnected modern economies. Additionally, the study provides methodological insights for analyzing dependent-variable scenarios in economic research, offering a robust computational approach for testing economic theories of sectoral behavior.

title: A Generalized Decomposition Framework for Multistate Life Expectancies
abstract: Multistate models are useful for analyzing life course dynamics across diverse domains, from family formation and labor market mobility to inter-regional migration and health trajectories. While recent methodological advances allow researchers to decompose differences in state expectancies into compositional and transition effects, existing method face critical limitations: they often obscure the distinct role of absorbing states (e.g., mortality) and are restricted to time-invariant population subgroups as composition. This study introduces a generalized decomposition framework within the Multiple Multistate Method (MMM) that resolves these constraints. By structurally separating survival from state transitions and accommodating time-varying covariates, the proposed method offers a flexible tool for disentangling the drivers of group disparities in any dynamic system. We illustrate the framework’s versatility using data from the Health and Retirement Study to decompose gaps in health expectancy driven by mortality, education, and changing morbidity profiles. Crucially, the applicability of this extended framework extends far beyond health. It provides a robust analytical strategy for understanding other state expectancy, such as marital expectancy and socioeconomic mobility, allowing researchers to isolate the specific mechanisms driving differences in multistate outcomes.

title: It Takes Two to Trust: Multi-modal Experimental Evidence of News Creators’ Influence on Trust-Based Decisions
abstract: While news trust declines across democracies, how news creators’ characteristics affect trust-based decisions, such as the choice to use news to inform important decisions about one’s health or finances, is still unclear. Through two conjoint experiments, a textual and visual one, we study how a news creator’s personal characteristics, sources of legitimacy, and relational practices aimed at showing audience recognition affect participants' trust-based choices in Italy. The visual experiment, which reproduces a “first-impression” scenario on Instagram, shows that a news creator’s gender, age, popularity, and professional label affect trust-based choices more than their journalistic practices signaling (1) a propensity to disclose editorial processes, (2) ease of accessibility, (3) attentiveness, and (4) responsiveness (or the lack thereof) to the needs of their audience. Yet, when this information is provided in a textual setting, implying consistent and long-standing engagement with these practices, they overwhelmingly regulate trust dynamics, overshadowing other characteristics.

title: Worry, efficacy and complexity: how the public thinks about the issue salience of climate change
abstract: Abstract Public support for climate action is broad, yet effective climate policy remains rare. Part of the explanation is that climate change is low in relative salience — people want action but prefer governments to prioritise other issues. Research has investigated the external drivers of issue salience, but the internal considerations shaping people's issue rankings remain poorly understood, with most accounts equating salience with worry. Drawing on reflexive thematic analysis of eight focus groups in New Zealand and Australia, this study investigates how members of the public think about the relative importance of climate change. Worry shaped rankings for many, but two further factors were identified: complexity and efficacy. Low efficacy — both internal and external — led participants to deprioritise climate change even when concerned about it, a link not previously identified in the literature. Climate salience is thus shaped not only by worry, but by perceptions of whether meaningful action is possible

title: Exploring factors for predicting the deterrent effect of darkness on cycling in urban areas
authors: Demet Yesiltepe, Steve Fotios, Maan Balela, Jim Uttley
abstract: In this work we use Odds Ratios (ORs) to establish the impact of darkness on the number of cyclists across 117 locations in five cities (Arlington (VA, USA), Bergen (Norway), Berlin (Germany), Birmingham and Leeds (UK)) using data from automated counters. We considered four reasons to explain the variance in ORs between counter locations: lighting condition (whether lit or unlit), the proportion of recreational journeys, distance from the city centre and the number of cyclists. The results suggest that lighting condition had the greatest influence, followed by the proportion of recreational journeys and the number of cyclists. The model developed to predict ORs explained 65% of the variance.

title: Boundary Changes and Competitive Seat Exclusion in Singapore’s 2025 Redistricting: An Ensemble-Based Test
abstract: We test whether Singapore's 2025 electoral redistricting produced a statistically anomalous allocation of competitively contested territory. Using a hypergeometric permutation test, we examine the 114 subzones that changed constituency between the 2020 and 2025 general elections. Among the 52 subzones originating from competitive 2020 constituencies (PAP vote share < 55%), zero ended up in competitive 2025 constituencies—against an expected value of 3.2 under random redistribution (Fisher’s exact p = 0.012). The anomaly follows a specific pattern: the new competitive seat (Jalan Kayu SMC) was seeded from stronghold populations, while dissolved competitive constituencies were absorbed into safe districts. A complementary MCMC ensemble analysis using GerryChain finds that while Singapore’s boundaries are extreme outliers in geometric compactness, this is consistent with administrative logic rather than partisan gerrymandering. We conclude that while the GRC block-vote remains the primary seat-to-vote bonus mechanism, redistricting serves as a secondary mechanism for managing competitive communities.

title: A Systematic Review of Sport for Development and Peace Reviews
authors: Hozhabri, Umair Asif, Louis-Charles Pitre, Karen Petry, Simona Safarikova, Jean Jacques Rondeau, Paquito Bernard, Tegwen Gadais
abstract: Introduction: In the last decade, Sport for Development (SFD) and Peace (SDP) reviews have seen an increase in published review research to explore the potential of sport as a vehicle for development to attain Sustainable Development Goals among others. Some reviews have been conducted on several related themes, but to date, no attempt has been made to evaluate and scrutinize review contributions in this field. The aim of this study is to conduct a systematic review of SFD/SDP reviews to provide a complete and updated picture of the field of research. Methods: A scoping-systematic review using PRISMA-ScR guidelines was used to identify reviews published on SFD/SDP since 2000. After applying strict inclusion criteria in 8 languages, including grey literature, 21 reviews were identified through database searches in Scopus, SPORTDiscus, ScieLO, SocINDEX, ProQuest, PsyCINFO and Google Scholar. Experts from the field were consulted, mapping of authors and overlapping of studies were conducted and the quality of reviews was assessed. Findings: Findings highlighted diverse thematic focuses, including those aligned with SDGs such as health promotion, gender equality, and educational outcomes, alongside novel areas such as managerial implications and critical pedagogy. Despite the wealth of research, consensus on sport’s developmental potential remains elusive, hindered by methodological limitations and a dearth of quality assessments. Moreover, the dominance of English publications underscores a need for linguistic inclusivity, while reevaluating terminology and expanding disciplinary engagement can enrich and globalize the discourse. Critical reflection is warranted on the historical context of SFD/SDP, moving beyond colonial narratives to embrace diverse development paradigms, including intra-European perspectives. Conclusion: The field of SFD/SDP seems to need initiatives that focus on bottom-up approaches, with long-term monitoring, evaluation of changes over time, attention to cultural contexts and local participation. It also necessitates a rigorous revision of methodologies to better elucidate the multi-level impact of sport-based initiatives on achieving developmental objectives, and to bring in the viewpoints of other disciplines to gain a more complex understanding of the SFD/SDP field.

title: Factors influencing meal composition in university restaurant settings: a multi-agent simulation approach
authors: Nicolas Darcel, Patrick Taillandier, Olga Davidenko, Aurélie Maurice, Damien Saveant
abstract: This study aims to uncover the diverse factors that influence meal composition decisions in university restaurant settings. To achieve this, we employed a combination of agent-based modeling and in-situ measurements of meal choices made by guests in natural consumption situations. Our model considered individual preferences for presented dishes, social influence from other agents in the simulated environment, and environmental factors such as sensory perceptions and waiting time. Simultaneously, we conducted observations in two university restaurants during lunchtime, recording the meal choices of 336 students. Anthropometric data and subjective decision criteria were collected through self-reported questionnaires, and the composition of each guest's table was recorded as a proxy for familiarity between individuals. By utilizing the measured characteristics of the participants as initial parameters, we simulated choices and compared them with real participant data, allowing us to determine the importance of preferences, social influences, and environmental factors in decision-making. Despite the majority of respondents claiming no influence from others, our best fit models indicated a significant role for social influence in agents' decisions. Moreover, our model exploration revealed that not all dishes are equally susceptible to social influences, with variations in this parameter affecting different meal components differently. This study demonstrates how a multi-agent simulation approach can effectively characterize the influencing factors involved in complex decision-making problems and uncover mechanisms during meal composition in natural consumption situations.

title: Re-SearchTerms: A Shiny app for exploring terminology variations in psychology and metascience
authors: Anna Yi Leung, Daniel Kristanto, Xenia Schmalz
abstract: The wording of definitions for terms in open scholarship and research practices may be inconsistent, making it difficult for researchers to understand and apply them accurately. This hinders clear and effective communication in research. To address this, we developed “Re-SearchTerms”, a Shiny app that encourages researchers to revisit and critically examine the meanings of common research terms related to open scholarship and research practice used in diverse research contexts. This interactive platform enables the exploration and comparison of definitions from multiple sources, prompting users to reflect on the nuanced meanings and contexts of key terms. Our dataset includes 2211 definitions of 216 concepts related to open scholarship, research practices, statistical analysis, and philosophy-of-science terminology. Definitions are sourced from academic texts, the crowdsourced FORRT glossary (Parsons et al., 2022), and Wiktionary (Meyer, 2018). Using natural language processing (NLP) techniques, Re-SearchTerms visualises term relationships based on word co-occurrences and sentence embeddings, allowing users to investigate co-occurrence patterns and explore concept-level networks. Moreover, we identify concepts with high and low semantic consistencies based on their definitions. Low similarity suggests variability in how researchers define and potentially understand a given term. This finding underscores the need for researchers to pay closer attention to the use of concepts, especially those with low consistency. Our app helps researchers make deliberate and informed choices when defining and interpreting key terms. Our adaptive framework can be applied to other datasets of research concepts, enabling researchers to examine and select terminology, especially in interdisciplinary fields.

title: May I Help You? The Relationship Between Interpersonal Emotion Regulation and Emotional and Relational Wellbeing in Daily Life
authors: Lisanne Pauw, Sun Rui, Giulia Zoppolat, Francesca Righetti, Anne Milek
abstract: People often get support from others in regulating their emotions, a phenomenon known as interpersonal emotion regulation (IER). However, the relative effectiveness of specific IER strategies for improving emotional and relational wellbeing in daily life is unclear. Here, we report two preregistered, ecological momentary assessment studies, in which we examined how the use of six key IER strategies relates to emotional and relational wellbeing among romantic couples in daily life. Study 1 focused on enacted IER as reported by the regulator, whereas Study 2 focused on perceived IER as reported by the regulated partner. Using an experience sampling design (6 beeps/day for 7 days), Study 1 (N = 136) showed that when people reported to have given advice or encouraged their partner to suppress their emotions, their partners experienced impaired emotional wellbeing. When people reported to have distracted their partner, their partner experienced enhanced positive affect and felt closer to their partner. The use of interpersonal reappraisal, acceptance and ignoring was unrelated to partners’ momentary wellbeing. Using a daily diary design (1 beep/day for 12 days), Study 2 (N = 361) showed that perceptions of one’s emotions being ignored by the partner were associated with impaired emotional and relational wellbeing on the same day. The perceived use of other IER strategies was unrelated to momentary wellbeing. Taken together, the present set of studies illuminates how IER processes shape people’s emotions and relationships in ecologically valid settings. Our findings indicate that enacted and perceived regulatory behaviors are associated with differential outcomes, highlighting the complex nature of interpersonal emotion dynamics.

title: The Role of Migration History and Household Structure in Internal Migration: Evidence from the Netherlands, 1850-1940
authors: Andrea Colasurdo, Clara H. Mulder, Diego Alburez-Gutierrez
abstract: This study investigates the role of kin in shaping migration behavior, focusing on how the migration history of an individual’s parents, their own past migration experience, and the presence of household members is associated with the individual’s likelihood and frequency of internal migration over time. Utilizing the Historical Sample of the Netherlands, which provides comprehensive migration histories of individuals from 1850 to 1940 and detailed information on the household composition at each address, we analyze internal migrations throughout the life course for multiple generations. Our findings suggest that individuals with at least one parent who had a migration background—defined as having migrated within the Netherlands—were more likely to move internally, indicating the intergenerational transmission of migration behavior. Having migrated together with parents earlier in life was also associated with a higher likelihood of migrating without parents later on. Parents’ migration histories and individuals’ own migration experience with parents were associated with higher frequency of internal migrations made without parents. Finally, living with a spouse, at least one parent, or extended kin was associated with lower likelihood of migrating. This work contributes to our understanding of migration patterns from a historical perspective, highlighting the long-term role of family networks and family migration experiences on individuals’ migration behavior.

title: Beyond counting trees: considering actors’ demands for more inclusive and effective restoration
authors: Estelle Raveloaritiana, Dominic A. Martin, O. Sarobidy Rakotonarivo, James Herrera, Manoa Rajaonarivelo, Thio Rosin Fulgence, Hery Lisy Tiana Ranarijaona, Ando Nantenaina Andrianarivelo, Mendrika Famenontsoa Randrianarivelo, Linjasoa Nantenaina Rakotomalala
abstract: Global calls for more inclusive restoration highlight the need to consider multiple actors including local communities, practitioners, policymakers and donors, who all have different demands. Yet, restoration rarely integrates these demands. We propose the Demand4Restoration framework, a demand-oriented approach in which actors’ demands explicitly guide restoration evaluation and planning. In evaluation, actors' demands define what to assess, how, against which benchmarks, for whose benefit, and which drivers to test. In planning, they inform where to restore, for what purpose, how, by whom, and under what conditions. Demand4Restoration clarifies alignments and mismatches across actors, improving social legitimacy and sustainability of restoration.

title: A Market for Preprints: Knowledge Accessibility, Execution Leakage, and the Economics of Academic Disclosure in the Age of AI
abstract: Research fields differ in knowledge accessibility — the degree to which a field’s questions and execution methods are legible to trained non-specialists. We develop a sequential game-theoretic model of preprint disclosure in which researcher A chooses whether to declare an idea; if she declares, researcher B learns the idea and executes with probability λ, the execution leakage rate. The declarer retains residual priority share α; the rival incurs execution cost ce. A single cutoff λc separates universal disclosure from strategic opacity. Generative AI raises λ heterogeneously — sharply in high-accessibility fields where execution requires only codified knowledge, negligibly where execution requires tacit expertise or physical capital AI cannot substitute. Heterogeneous idea value generates adverse selection: as λ rises, the disclosure cutoff π∗ rises, withdrawing high-value ideas while AI-lowered production costs sustain a flood of lower-value declarations — the compositional flood. Using OpenAlex data across seven fields from 2019 to 2024, we document a structural break: low-accessibility fields (mathematics, physics, chemistry) decelerate post-ChatGPT (mean −1.22 pp/year) while high-accessibility fields (economics, sociology) accelerate sharply (+1.51 pp/year) — a differential of 2.73 pp/year coinciding precisely with the AI transition. We identify a new pre-publication anti-commons in the tradition of Murray & Stern (2007) and discuss implications for preprint platform design and open science policy.

title: The Dynamics of Population Mixing: A Simulation Approach
abstract: The population processes involved in the growth of a self-identified multiracial population are complex and depend on a variety of social factors not easily captured in standard demographic projection methods. In this article, we use a microsimulation approach of a two group population to explore how variation in relative group size, the frequency of intergroup partnering, and the probability of self-identification all contribute to the growth of a self-identified multiracial population over a 200 year simulation period. The results show that under most realistic scenarios, the tendency for mixed ancestry lineages to fold back into single race identification leads to modest growth in the multiracial population compared to what could be possible. Furthermore, in cases of unequal group size, the most liberal scenarios can lead to the growth of the majority population at the expense of both the minority and multiracial population.

title: Awareness and Perception of Ethical Issues in Modern Science among Undergraduate Students: A Survey-Based Study
abstract: The accelerated growth of science and technology has intensified ethical concerns related to research practices, data governance, and societal impact. Understanding how undergraduate students perceive these issues is essential, as they represent the future scientific workforce. This study investigates the awareness and perceptions of ethical issues in modern science among undergraduate students. A structured questionnaire was administered online using Google Forms, and participation was voluntary and anonymous. A total of 50 undergraduate students from diverse academic backgrounds responded. The survey examined three domains: awareness of ethical issues, perceptions of ethical challenges in scientific advancement, and attitudes toward ethics education. The findings indicate that most students demonstrate a foundational awareness of ethical concerns and express strong support for integrating ethics into academic curricula. However, responses also reveal uncertainty regarding the adequacy of existing ethical frameworks in addressing modern challenges. The study underscores the need for structured ethics education to foster responsible scientific practices.

title: Moral and Ethical Orientations and Responses to Nonpharmaceutical Public Health Recommendations in the Early COVID-19 Pandemic
abstract: During the early COVID-19 pandemic, citizens of the United States were asked to comply with strict nonpharmaceutical public health recommendations to stop the spread of the virus. As the response evolved, resistance to these recommendations began to emerge along moral and ethical lines. While a growing body of post-pandemic research examines moral associations with public health compliance, no studies have examined the ethical orientations that existed prior to large-scale politicization of the pandemic response. This study is a cross-sectional secondary analysis of a convenience sample (N=110) collected in March 2020, before mask mandates and widespread political polarization of the public health response. Participants completed Forsyth's (1980) Ethics Position Questionnaire and indicated agreement or disagreement with six nonpharmaceutical public health recommendations. Analysis was triangulated using Pearson's correlation, independent samples ttest, and one-way ANOVA. Findings reveal a "Golden Hour" of broad consensus with collective public health recommendations during the early pandemic, with no statistically significant differences across Forsyth's (1980) four ethical taxonomies. Exploratory trends that did not survive Bonferroni correction suggest an association between idealism and disagreement with the recommendation that senior citizens stay home, and between relativism and agreement that those with serious underlying health conditions should stay home. These findings suggest that collective public health recommendations can achieve high levels of compliance through uniform messaging; however, targeted, individualized recommendations require ethically segmented communication strategies. The Ethical Activation Messaging Model (EAMM) is proposed as a theoretical framework to guide future research and public health communication practice.

title: An Open-Source Smartphone Screen-Recording Tool with Application to Political Science Research
authors: Daniel Muise, Justin Cornelius, Jihye Lee, Yingdan Lu, Jacob N Shapiro
abstract: Political scientists seek to understand the political environment and to identify causal relationships within it. This often requires accurate measurement of how individuals consume political information. Many data-collection approaches, particularly those relying on social media content, infer consumption behavior from publicly posted material. Yet public-facing, platform-specific content misrepresents the fragmented, multimedia nature of smartphone use, and thus of political information consumption. Continuous smartphone screen-recording (CSSR) offers a theoretically strong means of capturing individuals’ information experience, but has remained largely inaccessible to political scientists due to technical, operational, analytical, and ethical barriers. We introduce an open-source CSSR tool to mitigate these challenges. We motivate its relevance for political science and outline its solutions to practical constraints that have limited CSSR’s adoption.

title: The hidden burden: Mental health among people affected by neglected tropical diseases in Nigeria
authors: Ruth Abioye, Rasaq Kayode Oladapo, Michael Olawale Olaoyo, Usoro Akpan, Oluwatosin Rachael Afolabi
abstract: Neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) affect over one billion people globally, disproportionately impacting populations in low-resource settings such as Nigeria. While the physical consequences of NTDs are well documented, their mental health burden remains under-recognised and insufficiently integrated into policy and practice. Individuals affected by NTDs frequently experience depression, anxiety, stigma, and reduced quality of life, driven by interconnected pathways including disability, social exclusion, poverty, and cultural interpretations of disease. Current burden estimation frameworks often fail to capture these psychosocial dimensions, leading to underestimation of disease impact and misaligned health priorities. This Viewpoint argues for greater recognition of the mental health burden associated with NTDs and highlights the need for integrated, person-centred approaches to care. Addressing these gaps is essential for improving quality of life and strengthening progress toward NTD control and elimination.

title: Generic Frameworks for Ranked Fusion Voting
