abstract: We introduce two generic frameworks for ranked fusion voting: Tagged Ranked Fusion Voting (TRFV) and Line-Ranked Fusion Voting (LRFV). In TRFV, vot- ers rank candidates and assign one party tag to each ranked candidate; in LRFV, voters rank candidate–party lines directly. Both frameworks are modular rather than self-contained, and must therefore be instantiated with an underlying ranked voting rule on candidates. We formalize this architecture through a collapse operator that maps fusion ballots to the candidate-level inputs required by the underlying rule. For ranked rules based on candidate orderings, first-occurrence collapse provides a natural implementation. We present this collapse not as an exhaustive characterization of all admissible collapses, but as the simplest canonical construction satisfying the baseline constraints relevant to candidate- order-based ranked rules. We show that the resulting framework reduces to the underlying ranked rule when fusion is absent, is invariant at the candidate level under intra-candidate party refinement, and embeds TRFV as a restricted special case of LRFV. The framework applies to both single-seat and multi-seat settings and accommodates party attribution without altering candidate-level selection.

title: Psilocybin use among Basotho healers and non-healers in southern Africa
authors: Eli Stark-Elster, Mamosebetsi Sethathi, Breyten van der Merwe, Sandeep M Nayak, David Bryce Yaden, Manvir Singh
abstract: Reliable documentation of the traditional use of serotonergic psychedelics has long been confined to the Americas. Prevailing narratives often overstate the global prevalence and uniformity of traditional psychedelic use, extrapolating from a limited set of well-documented cases. Here, we report evidence of psilocybin mushroom use among Basotho traditional healers and non-healers in Lesotho and South Africa. Through semi-structured interviews with 26 healers (one subsequently excluded) and 8 non-healers, we found that 15 healers and 6 non-healers independently identified Psilocybe maluti, a recently described psilocybin-producing species endemic to southern Africa, and reported uses spanning four categories: incorporation into a psychoactive initiation brew, treatment of physical, mental, and spiritual ailments, recreation, and magical protection. Unlike the large ritualized doses characteristic of Mesoamerican psilocybin use, Basotho healers primarily apply small doses of P. maluti alongside other psychoactive plants, most notably the hallucinogenic bulb Boophone disticha. Multiple lines of evidence suggest these practices predate the mid-twentieth-century popularization of psilocybin. These findings expand the known geographic scope of traditional psilocybin use and reveal a mode of psychedelic application distinct from existing ethnographic models. More broadly, they underscore the importance of grounding contemporary interpretations of non-clinical psychedelic practices in careful empirical documentation rather than widely circulated but weakly supported generalizations.

title: Beyond Financial Incentives: Family Life Education and Fertility Literacy as Policy Tools for Sustainable Demographics
abstract: Objective: This study investigates how socio-economic factors, cultural norms, and formal education shape fertility intentions in pronatalist contexts. It particularly evaluates the current state and potential priority of Family Life Education (FLE) within school curricula as a sustainable policy instrument. Background: Despite extensive pronatalist interventions, many high-income nations continue to experience persistent sub-replacement fertility. Although traditional policies rely heavily on financial transfers, evidence suggests that structural socio-economic factors often override these incentives. This paper argues that addressing the fertility gap requires a strategic shift from purely economic measures toward formal institutional interventions in the form of educational policy. Method: Utilizing a comparative policy analysis framework, the study examines six distinct national contexts: the United States, Norway, Ireland, Hungary, South Korea, and China. The analysis maps the integration (or lack thereof) of Family Life Education (FLE) within national curricula and evaluates its alignment with demographic goals. Results: The findings reveal that a primary obstacle to effective pronatalist outcomes is the fragmented and non-mandatory nature of Family Life Education across the examined countries. Conclusion: The study concludes that for pronatalist policies to be more effective, Family Life Education (FLE) should be strengthened and better integrated into formal education systems. Policy Implications: Policymakers should adopt a comprehensive approach that recognizes cash transfers and financial incentives as essential components of pronatalist policy. At the same time, establishing Family Life Education (FLE) as a formal, standalone component of the school curriculum would significantly strengthen these efforts. By combining robust financial support with systematic educational interventions, countries can more effectively address both the economic and cultural roots of sub-replacement fertility, while fostering informed reproductive decision-making and long-term demographic sustainability.

title: Rebuilding the Workforce: Addressing the Construction Skills Crisis in Nigeria Through Technical and Vocational Education
abstract: Nigeria’s construction sector, one of the largest in Sub-Saharan Africa, is confronted by a deepening crisis of skilled labour that threatens the nation’s ambitious infrastructure development agenda. Rapid urbanisation, a growing housing deficit estimated at 22 million units, and a surge in public infrastructure projects have exposed severe shortcomings in the technical capacity of the construction workforce. This article investigates the structural roots of the skills crisis in Nigeria’s construction industry, examines the current state of Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) as a remedial mechanism, and evaluates pathways for reform. Drawing on empirical research, policy documents and industry reports, the article argues that systematic reform of Nigeria’s TVET sector, encompassing curriculum modernisation, increased government funding, private sector engagement and the professionalisation of technical education, is both urgent and achievable. The article further highlights the critical and widely underappreciated role of Building Technology educators in creating a self-sustaining pipeline of competent construction professionals. Recommendations are offered for policymakers, construction industry stakeholders and educational institutions committed to transforming Nigeria’s built environment.

title: Algorithm Literacy: A Short Conceptual Overview
abstract: Algorithm literacy—the capacity to understand, critically evaluate, and respond to algorithmic systems—has become a key concept in communication and media studies. This text offers a concise conceptual overview, covering the core dimensions of algorithm literacy, its development through experiential learning and folk theories, and the interplay of endogenous and exogenous factors shaping it. The text discusses the concept's relevance for civic participation, platform labor, and collective mobilization, while critically examining its limits: uneven social distribution, systemic opacity, and the risk that framing algorithmic competence as "literacy" shifts responsibility from institutions to individuals. The text is the original English-language version of a lexicon entry published in Danish in the Medie- og kommunikationsleksikon (Samfundslitteratur, 2025).

title: Authentic content as an effective mechanism for embedding patient voices: a hypothesis and conceptual framework
abstract: Health innovation systems continue to face persistent inefficiencies, mistrust, and escalating development costs despite rapid scientific progress. Although patient-engagement is increasingly recognised as essential for patient-centred healthcare innovation, there is limited conceptual and empirical research explaining how patient voices can be systematically embedded within processes and it is relatively unexplored in literature how this influences measurable outcomes such as engagement, collaboration, trust and return-on-investment. This paper addresses this gap through a hypothesis and conceptual analysis informed by interdisciplinary literature, industry reports and case-based evidence examining the integration of patient voices across health innovation processes and posits that authentic content functions as an effective and scalable mechanism for embedding patient voices across multiple stages of the health innovation lifecycle. The emergence of artificial intelligence also offers the potential to increase and amplify diffusions from authentic content and facilitate mass personalisation of patient voices across innovation lifecycles but, even without this, authentic content should be considered as a "hard" strategic imperative and a high-fidelity mechanism for multi-level stakeholder engagement, enhanced patient access and stronger financial outcomes. The timing of authentic content deployment is also identified as a critical determinant of its transformational potential, with earlier integration resulting in a "leftward shift" in the cumulative and multiplicative trajectory of stakeholder-engagement and value-creation as well as an acceleration of progression of development that results in considerably longer periods of effective commercial market exclusivity, more patients being able to access medicines faster, and providers being able to offer more efficient access as a result of greater scalability. If validated through future empirical research, authentic content may represent a scalable and measurable approach for embedding patient voices as a strategic driver of engagement, innovation efficiency, and patient-centred value creation in health innovation systems.

title: Behind the Scenes: Online Food Ordering
abstract: Online food ordering has emerged as a significant digital food determinant with direct implications for offline food consumption patterns among young adults. This qualitative study examines how online food ordering applications influence food decision-making and consumption behaviour in New Zealand young adults aged 18 to 25 years. Semi-structured face-to-face interviews were conducted with 15 participants, with data analysed using inductive thematic analysis following the Braun and Clarke framework. Three major themes were identified: reasons for downloading online food ordering applications (monetary and non-monetary); impact on consumption behaviour; and the relationship between application interface design and food ordering choices. Findings indicate that application interfaces, promotional incentives, and convenience factors drive increased food ordering frequency and larger order volumes than intended. Notably, no participants reported considering the healthiness of food when ordering online. The study highlights the habit-forming nature of online-to-offline (O2O) food ordering and raises concerns about its potential contribution to energy-dense, nutrition-poor dietary patterns in young adults — a demographic already identified as vulnerable to poor food choices. These findings contribute to the emerging literature on digital food environments and their role as neo-food determinants.

title: Kommunale Bürgerumfrage 2023
authors: Stadt Leipzig, Amt für Statistik und Wahlen
abstract: Bei der Kommunalen Bürgerumfrage handelt es sich um eine Mehrthemenbefragung (Omnibusbefragung). Die inhaltlichen Schwerpunkte ergeben sich aus den Informations­bedürfnissen der Stadtverwaltung. Der vorliegende Ergebnisbericht wertet ausgewählte Fragestellungen der Kommunalen Bürgerumfrage 2023 grafisch und textlich aus.

title: Victory Without End: Temporal Decoupling and the Transformation of Contemporary Warfare
abstract: Contemporary warfare is increasingly characterised by the coexistence of contradictory claims, including declarations of victory alongside ongoing military operations. Existing approaches, particularly those focused on simulation and political communication, explain how narratives stabilise rapidly under conditions of uncertainty, but do not fully account for why contradiction persists. This article addresses this gap by introducing temporal decoupling as a conceptual framework for understanding the transformation of war under conditions of speed. Drawing on Baudrillard’s account of simulation and Virilio’s analysis of acceleration, it argues that contemporary conflict is organised through a compression of time in which events, claims, and actions no longer unfold sequentially but emerge simultaneously within a shared temporal frame. Under these conditions, contradiction is not resolved but persists as a structural feature of warfare. The article develops this argument through a qualitative, interpretive analysis of the March 2026 escalation involving the United States and Iran, examining how claims of victory, escalation, and resolution circulate across media, political, and digital environments. It advances the argument across four interconnected dimensions: the role of simulation in early narrative stabilisation; the production of simultaneity through speed; the temporal decoupling of action, outcome, and interpretation; and the spatial reconfiguration of conflict as a contested “right to the air.” The article shows that acceleration not only disrupts the temporal sequencing of war but also reshapes its spatial organisation, producing a form of conflict in which victory no longer marks closure and war is experienced as a continuous, overlapping, and distributed field of action.

title: The Algorithmic Consensus Hypothesis: Systemic Risk in the Age of Uniform AI Driven Markets
abstract: This paper introduces and develops the Algorithmic Consensus Hypothesis (ACH), which argues that the proliferation of machine learning-driven investment strategies among institutional investors has produced measurable convergence in factor exposures, signal consumption, and portfolio positioning, generating a new and underappreciated class of systemic risk. Unlike traditional contagion channels rooted in balance sheet linkages or funding structures, algorithmic consensus operates through epistemic homogeneity: when competing agents train on overlapping alternative data, adopt similar neural architectures, and optimize against shared benchmark signals, their strategies cohere silently and without coordination. Drawing on evidence from documented quant crowding episodes, cross-sectional return co-movement anomalies, and the theoretical literature on information cascades and reflexivity, the paper argues that market diversity, long treated as an emergent property of competitive heterogeneity, is now an engineered outcome that markets may be systematically eroding. A conceptual empirical framework is proposed, utilizing hedge fund 13-F filings, factor model residuals, and shock-response correlation analysis to test convergence directly. The paper concludes that regulatory attention to model diversity, not merely to capital and leverage, is increasingly urgent, and that alpha decay in quantitative strategies may be the market's early warning signal that consensus has already arrived.

title: Health Expenditure and Life Expectancy: Cross-Country Panel Evidence on the Moderating Role of Education and Infrastructure
abstract: This paper revisits the relationship between health expenditure and life expectancy by distinguishing between cross-country moderation patterns and within-country temporal evidence. Using cross-sectional data for 111 countries in 2019 alongside two-way fixed effects panel models covering 2010–2019 with one-year lagged covariates, the study examines whether adult literacy and electricity access moderate the health expenditure–longevity association. In cross-sectional data, the association between health expenditure and life expectancy is more pronounced in countries with higher literacy and greater electricity access, consistent with a complementarity hypothesis. These interaction patterns are not recovered with statistical precision in within-country fixed effects models. Adult literacy and electricity access remain positively associated with within-country changes in life expectancy, but the health expenditure coefficient is imprecise in the panel setting, reflecting limited within-country annual variation in spending after absorbing country fixed effects. The findings are more consistent with a structural and long-run interpretation of these complementarities than with a short-run within-country dynamic, while also highlighting the limits of short-panel identification for slow-moving national characteristics.

title: Informational Power and Institutional Design: When a Hegemon May Choose Consensus
abstract: When and why would a hegemon prefer consensus to majority rule in an international organization? I argue that consensus can be a technology of hegemonic power rather than a constraint upon it. In a formal model, a hegemon privately observes the value of cooperation and uses Bayesian persuasion to influence weaker states' entry decisions. Under majority rule, weak states can bypass the hegemon, eliminating any screening problem. Under unanimity, weak states must include the hegemon in every bargaining coalition without knowing its type, creating a screening cutoff at which their behavior changes discretely. This generates an upward jump in the hegemon's value function---a non-convexity that Bayesian persuasion exploits. The institutional comparison has a single-crossing property: there is a unique prior threshold, in closed form, above which the hegemon prefers unanimity and below which majority can dominate by making entry easier. The mechanism reframes institutional design as a trade-off between informational leverage under unanimity and easier participation under majority, providing a distributive explanation for consensus rules in organizations like the GATT/WTO.

title: The Addictive Nexus and Beliefs Among Uruguayan Pathological Gamblers: A Deductive Qualitative Study
abstract: Background. Pathological gambling is a growing mental health problem that requires appropriate prevention and intervention strategies. In Uruguay, prevalence studies have indicated an increase in this problem. Objective. This study seeks to understand the beliefs that sustain the addictive connection in people with problematic gambling behaviors, using a deductive qualitative approach. Methods. Seven adult gamblers at high risk for addiction, residing in rural areas of Uruguay, were interviewed by telephone: four women and three men, with an average age of 44 years and more than 15 years of gambling involvement. A deductive analysis of their narratives was conducted. Results. The results confirm theoretical assumptions that emphasize biases linked to control as central to the addictive relationship. Additionally, beliefs legitimizing gambling—gambling for fun, escape, or compensation—and other findings that qualitatively enrich the understanding of the phenomenon are reported. Conclusion. This work provides emerging data on the subjective experiences of at-risk Uruguayan gamblers and highlights the need to continue deepening our understanding of this issue. The findings have relevant implications for mental health prevention and intervention.

title: Pricing the Future: China’s Ambitions for Commodity Derivatives Markets
abstract: China’s efforts to develop commodity futures markets and enhance its influence over commodity pricing are best understood as part of a broader strategy to reduce financial vulnerabilities and increase strategic autonomy within a hierarchical global financial system. Commodity pricing power – defined as the capacity to shape the infrastructures, regulation, participation and currency denomination of derivative markets through which prices are formed – has become an increasingly important component of geopolitical competition. For China, this is not about economic efficiency, but control over the terms under which strategically important commodities are valued and traded.

title: Triangulating Friction Points in the Fentanyl Supply Chain
authors: Maura Austin, Nathan Timbs, Philip Potter
abstract: After more than a decade and billions of dollars spent combatting the worst opioid crisis in US history, the CDC documented a striking decline in fentanyl- related mortality beginning in May 2023. What drove this decline? We synthesize three sources of data – global shipping records, fentanyl seizures, and overdose deaths – to serve as empirical signals through which we gain visibility into critical links in the supply chain. This framework allows us to triangulate where in the supply chain the disruption originated. Our analysis indicates that the decline in overdose deaths stemmed in substantial part from enforcement operations that destabilized Mexican cartels’ production and trafficking networks, alongside any effects of precursor controls, domestic enforcement, and harm-reduction efforts.

title: The Workplace Grief Readiness Standard: An Evidence-Based Framework for Corporate Bereavement Response
abstract: Most corporate bereavement policy is not built on evidence. It is built on repetition, and repetition has been mistaken for proof. The modern organizational conversation about workplace grief rests on a small set of widely repeated statistics, several of which are misattributed, methodologically opaque, or entirely unrelated to grief. The most commonly cited figure, $225.8 billion in annual employer losses attributed to grief, originates from a 2003 productivity audit covering all health conditions and was reframed as a grief-specific cost by a CDC Foundation press release more than a decade later. Other figures that anchor HR discourse, including a claimed 91 percent productivity drop and a manager identification rate of only 11 percent, trace to vendor reports and trade blog posts with no disclosed methodology. These figures are not credible enough to inform policy decisions. They persist because they are easy to repeat, not because they are valid. This paper does not argue that workplace grief is overstated. It argues that the evidence used to justify reform is often misused. A defensible peer-reviewed evidence base, developed through neuroimaging studies, prospective longitudinal cohorts, and rigorous meta-analyses, demonstrates that bereavement affects cognition, physical health, work functioning, and workforce equity in ways that are material to employers and organizationally mishandled at scale. This paper separates what the research actually supports from what is merely repeated in HR discourse and introduces the Workplace Grief Readiness Standard (WGRS), a five-dimension benchmark against which organizational bereavement response can be assessed and improved. It concludes by identifying three priority research gaps: bereavement-attributable turnover, evidence-based return-to-work protocols, and independent evaluation of employer grief-support platforms. The question is no longer whether organizations should respond to employee grief. The question is whether they are willing to do it competently.

title: Equity, Diversity, Inclusion, and Decolonization (EDID) (Best) Practices across Academic and Community Settings: Environmental Scan Report 2025
authors: Nicola M. Dove, Sandra Lapointe, Sheila Cote-Meek, Jessica Braimoh, Kevin Lamoreux, Maryam Mohiuddin Ahmed
abstract: This report details findings from an environmental scan and provides recommendations for implementing EDID team practices and processes, as we endeavour to establish campus-community partnerships and execute the partnered Impact Training for Social Innovation initiative.

title: Applying a Human Development Lens to Early Career Professionals’ Workplace Engagement and Citizenship Behaviors
authors: Meg Small, Sally Dancer, Jonathan Wright, Blake A. Colaianne, Gillian Sommerville
abstract: How might constructs from motivation and identity theories, in addition to traditional financial incentives, predict engagement and workplace citizenship behaviors among early career professionals? Human development theories of motivation and identity have predicted engagement and other positive behavioral outcomes in adjacent fields. A subset of data from a larger SPARC survey, which assessed individual and organizational factors contributing to workplace well-being and effectiveness, was used to answer two research questions: (1) What is the relationship between motivational factors derived from the self-determination theory, including authentic relationships, opportunities for growth, and alignment with values in predicting levels of employee engagement and workplace citizenship behaviors, and (2) Does a socialization identity predict employee engagement and workplace citizenship behaviors? Regression analyses were used to determine the relationship and overall model fit. Authentic relationships, opportunities for growth, and alignment with values predicted employee engagement. Financial rewards did not. The model explained 61% of the variance. All four variables were significant predictors of workplace citizenship with the model explaining 62% of the variance. A socialization identity also predicted both engagement and workplace citizenship. Findings can be used to develop and test novel programs and incentives that benefit firms’ performance and employee well-being simultaneously.

title: Co-Flourishing in Social Interaction: When Cooperation Is Experienced as Well-Being
abstract: Research on cooperation has demonstrated that individuals often engage in cooperative behavior even when such behavior conflicts with immediate self-interest. At the same time, research on well-being has emphasized flourishing as a form of positive functioning and meaningful engagement. Despite these advances, it remains theoretically unclear when cooperative interaction is itself experienced as well-being rather than as obligation, compliance, or strategic adaptation. This paper introduces the concept of co-flourishing, defined as a relational state in which cooperative behavior is accompanied by relationally grounded subjective well-being and expectations of relationship continuity. The central argument is that cooperation contributes to well-being only when it is experienced as self-congruent—that is, when individuals participate in interaction as agents rather than as passive or constrained actors. We develop a conceptual framework that specifies cooperative behavior, subjective experience, and relationship continuity as jointly necessary components of co-flourishing, and clarify self-congruence as a qualitative experiential condition shaping how cooperation is lived. To support conceptual clarity, the paper introduces the Relationship-Based Flourishing Index (RFI) as a theory-consistent operationalization illustrating how co-flourishing may be empirically examined without reducing it to any single behavioral or affective dimension. By focusing on cooperation as a lived relational process, the paper contributes to sociological and interdisciplinary discussions of well-being, agency, and social interaction.
